map.condition: Counting basic blocks in a 3-address code sequence
map.use: \text{Basic block starts at: (1) first instruction, (2) target of a jump, (3) instruction after a jump}

map.condition: Eliminating recomputed subexpressions within a block
map.use: \text{CSE: if } e \text{ was computed and operands unchanged, reuse the earlier temp}

map.condition: Moving code out of a loop that does not change across iterations
map.use: \text{Loop-invariant code motion: hoist statement } s \text{ if all operands of } s \text{ are loop-invariant}

map.condition: Minimum registers needed to evaluate expression tree without spilling
Register allocation by graph coloring — minimum colors (registers) needed
map.use: \text{Min registers} = \chi(G) \text{ where } G \text{ is the interference graph}

formulas.name: Leader Identification Rule
formulas.latex: \text{Leaders: } \begin{cases} (1) & \text{First instruction of the program} \\ (2) & \text{Target of any conditional/unconditional jump} \\ (3) & \text{Instruction immediately following a jump} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Leaders: (1) first instruction, (2) target of any jump, (3) instruction after any jump
formulas.whenToUse: Partitioning a 3-address code sequence into basic blocks

terms.means: first instruction of a basic block

formulas.trap: An instruction that is both a jump target AND follows a jump is still only ONE leader — don't double-count it as two blocks.

formulas.name: Basic Block Count Formula
formulas.whenToUse: After identifying all leader instructions, the count of leaders equals the count of blocks
formulas.trap: The last instruction being a jump does NOT create a new block after it unless another instruction follows.

formulas.name: DAG for a Basic Block
formulas.latex: \text{DAG nodes: one per distinct } (\text{operator, left-child, right-child}) \text{ triple}
formulas.plain: DAG: merge nodes with same operator and operands; leaves = input values/variables
formulas.whenToUse: Building a DAG to detect common subexpressions and dead code inside one basic block

terms.means: Directed Acyclic Graph representing computations in a block

formulas.trap: A variable that is reassigned gets a NEW leaf node in the DAG; the old node is no longer labeled with that variable.

formulas.name: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
formulas.latex: \text{If } t_1 = a \oplus b \text{ computed earlier and } a,b \text{ unchanged} \Rightarrow \text{replace recomputation by } t_1
formulas.plain: CSE: if t1 = a op b was computed before and a,b not redefined since, reuse t1 instead of recomputing
formulas.whenToUse: When the same expression appears multiple times in a basic block with no intervening redefinitions

terms.means: temporary holding the earlier computed value

formulas.trap: If ANY operand is reassigned between the two computations, CSE cannot be applied — even if the new value happens to be the same.

formulas.name: Constant Folding
formulas.latex: \text{If } x = c_1 \oplus c_2 \text{ where } c_1, c_2 \in \mathbb{Z} \Rightarrow \text{replace by } x = c_1 \oplus c_2 \text{ (evaluated at compile time)}
formulas.plain: Constant Folding: evaluate constant expressions at compile time (e.g. 4*5 -> 20)
formulas.whenToUse: Operands are both compile-time constants; avoids runtime arithmetic
formulas.trap: Floating-point constant folding may change semantics due to rounding — GATE questions usually stay with integers.

formulas.name: Constant Propagation
formulas.latex: \text{If } x = c \text{ and } x \text{ not redefined, replace later uses of } x \text{ by } c
formulas.plain: Constant Propagation: substitute the known constant value of x wherever x is used later
formulas.whenToUse: After an assignment x = constant, propagate c into subsequent uses of x in the same block

terms.means: the constant value assigned to x

formulas.trap: Propagation stops at any point where x is assigned a new value — even another constant.

formulas.name: Copy Propagation
formulas.latex: \text{If } x = y \text{ and } y \text{ unchanged, replace uses of } x \text{ by } y
formulas.plain: Copy Propagation: after x = y, substitute y for x in later uses (enabling dead code elimination)
formulas.whenToUse: When a variable is simply copied from another; often enables subsequent dead-code elimination
formulas.trap: Both x AND y must be unmodified for the substitution to be valid at a given use point.

formulas.name: Dead Code Elimination
formulas.latex: \text{Remove statement } x = e \text{ if } x \text{ is never used after this point (dead variable)}
formulas.plain: Dead Code Elimination: delete any assignment whose LHS variable is never subsequently read
formulas.whenToUse: After other optimizations (CSE, copy propagation) create assignments that are no longer referenced

terms.means: variable assigned but never used afterward

formulas.trap: An assignment is dead only if x is not live at that point — check all paths, not just the sequential next line.

formulas.name: Strength Reduction
formulas.latex: x^2 \to x \times x, \quad 2x \to x + x, \quad x \times 2^k \to x \ll k
formulas.plain: Strength Reduction: replace expensive ops with cheaper ones (e.g. multiply by power-of-2 -> left shift)
formulas.whenToUse: Loop induction variable expressions with multiplication/division by constants
formulas.trap: 4*j in a loop should be tracked as an induction variable incremented by 4 each iteration — do not apply strength reduction only once.

formulas.name: Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)
formulas.latex: s: x = f(v_1, \ldots, v_k) \text{ is loop-invariant if all } v_i \text{ are loop-invariant or defined outside loop}
formulas.plain: LICM: a statement is loop-invariant if all its operands are defined outside the loop or are themselves loop-invariant; hoist it to the pre-header
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying computations that produce the same value on every iteration of a loop

terms.means: new block inserted before the loop header to hold hoisted code

formulas.trap: A variable defined inside the loop is NOT loop-invariant even if its assigned value happens to be a constant — you must trace definitions, not values.

formulas.name: Induction Variable Recognition
formulas.latex: i \text{ is a basic IV if } i \mathrel{+}= c \text{ (constant) each iteration.} \quad j \text{ is a derived IV if } j = c_1 \cdot i + c_2
formulas.plain: Basic IV: incremented by a constant each loop iteration. Derived IV: linear function of a basic IV (j = c1*i + c2)
formulas.whenToUse: Strength reduction in loops: replace j = c1*i+c2 by incrementing j by c1*step each iteration

terms.means: increment of i per loop iteration

formulas.trap: 5*i is a derived IV of i; after strength reduction, it becomes a new IV incremented by 5 — do NOT recompute 5*i each time.

formulas.name: Sethi-Ullman Register Number
formulas.latex: r(n) = \begin{cases} 1 & n \text{ is a leaf} \\ \max(r(l), r(r)) & r(l) \neq r(r) \\ r(l) + 1 & r(l) = r(r) \end{cases}
formulas.plain: r(n): registers needed at node n. Leaf=1. If children need different counts: max(r(left),r(right)). If equal: r(left)+1.
formulas.whenToUse: Finding minimum registers to evaluate an expression tree with a single accumulator (Ershov / Sethi-Ullman algorithm)

terms.means: minimum registers needed to evaluate subtree rooted at n
left and right children of node n

formulas.trap: For NON-commutative ops (like subtraction), the evaluation order matters — swapping children may give wrong code. Must check which child needs more registers first.

formulas.name: Interference Graph & Graph Coloring
formulas.latex: \text{Variables } x, y \text{ interfere iff they are simultaneously live.} \quad \text{Min registers} = \chi(G)
formulas.plain: Two variables interfere if both are live at some program point. Min registers = chromatic number of interference graph.
formulas.whenToUse: Global register allocation: model conflicts as a graph and color it with k colors (k = #registers)
formulas.trap: If chi(G) > k, spilling is required. GATE questions often ask whether a given code needs exactly k registers — check max clique size as a lower bound.

formulas.name: Live Variable at a Point
formulas.latex: x \text{ is live at point } p \iff \exists \text{ a path } p \to q \text{ with use of } x \text{ at } q \text{ and no def of } x \text{ before } q
formulas.plain: x is live at point p if there is a path from p to a use of x with no redefinition of x along that path
formulas.whenToUse: Liveness analysis for dead code elimination and interference graph construction

terms.means: later point where x is used

formulas.trap: Liveness is a BACKWARDS data-flow problem: propagate USE and DEF sets from program exit toward entry.

formulas.name: Liveness Data-Flow Equations
formulas.plain: OUT[B] = union of IN[S] for all successors S. IN[B] = USE[B] union (OUT[B] minus DEF[B]).
formulas.whenToUse: Computing live variables at each basic block boundary in a control-flow graph

terms.means: variables used before any definition in block B
variables definitely defined in block B before any use
live variables at entry/exit of block B

formulas.trap: Iterate until a fixed point is reached (IN/OUT sets stop changing). Starting with OUT[exit]=empty and iterating backwards is standard.

formulas.name: Register Spilling
formulas.latex: \text{Spill variable } x \text{ with lowest spill cost: } \text{cost}(x) = \frac{\text{uses/defs of } x}{\text{degree of } x \text{ in interference graph}}
formulas.plain: Spill cost heuristic: spill the variable with lowest (uses+defs)/degree ratio — most savings per color freed
formulas.whenToUse: When interference graph chromatic number exceeds available registers; choose which variable to store in memory

terms.means: number of edges (interferences) of the node in the interference graph

formulas.trap: After spilling x, the interference graph changes (x's edges removed) — may enable coloring of remaining nodes without further spills.

formulas.name: Peephole Optimization Window
formulas.latex: \text{Slide a small window over generated code; apply local rewrite rules within window}
formulas.plain: Peephole: inspect a small window (2-5 instructions) of generated code and replace with shorter/faster equivalent sequences
formulas.whenToUse: Post-code-generation local improvement: eliminate redundant loads/stores, dead instructions, algebraic simplifications

terms.means: small contiguous set of machine instructions examined at once

formulas.trap: Peephole works on MACHINE code (or close to it), not on intermediate code — it is the last step, not the first.

formulas.name: Redundant Load/Store Elimination
formulas.latex: \text{STORE } R \to M[x] \text{ followed by LOAD } M[x] \to R \Rightarrow \text{delete the LOAD}
formulas.plain: If a value is stored to memory then immediately loaded back into the same register, delete the load
formulas.whenToUse: Peephole window contains a store immediately followed by a load of the same memory location into the same register
formulas.trap: The memory location must NOT be aliased — if another pointer could reference M[x], the load may be necessary.

formulas.name: Aliasing and Optimization Safety
formulas.latex: \text{If } a[j] \text{ may alias } a[i+2] \text{, then CSE/copy-prop across array accesses is UNSAFE}
formulas.plain: Aliasing: two pointer/array expressions refer to the same memory. Any optimization that reorders or eliminates memory accesses is unsafe in the presence of aliasing.
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding whether inter-statement optimizations (CSE, copy propagation) are valid across pointer/array writes

terms.means: two different expressions denoting the same memory location

formulas.trap: In the classic GATE question, work2 stores a[j] before re-reading a[i+2] — if i+2==j, the read sees the updated value, so CSE of a[i+2] is incorrect.
